speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two, Lidia Eichenholz interview, November 2nd, 1995, Minneapolis, Minnesota. Did you-- going on-- did you see a Yiddish theater there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Yiddish theater used to come to Rowne. And I saw, besides as I mentioned before, plays with Kaminska, Ida Kaminska. I saw her only-- of the yes, it was-- but it was not made to a musical. It was as a play, which was very good. And there's a couple of others I don't. And Rowne was prosperous. People were buying the tickets, and shows were well-attended.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time at which Hitler invaded Poland in September 1st of--

speaker: interviewer
text: 1939, yes. We all remember that date. Where were you? What was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, we were vacationing in, in mountains and were absolutely unaware until we read in papers about Chamberlain and all the efforts of the war. And then we knew about Czechoslo-- Austria, Czechoslovakia. It was not taken seriously. Nobody believed he will come, he will do it, especially when Chamberlain came back and declared that peace for our time, that he reached agreement with Hitler whom he visited. We just took literally his words.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you thought there would be peace?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I mean, we kids were busy with our usual distractions, like hiking and swimming and, and nobody really took it seriously. So when-- don't-- that applied also to our parents and all the people. They just started to, to come and go. We became more serious when, when Ribbentrop and Molotov signed a pact, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Between Russia and?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between Russia and Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: But also, it was taken lightly.

speaker: interviewer
text: It didn't seem that anything would come of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Everybody said, well, the World War I was so fallacious. So why would they want? We were unaware of, of Germany being armed to the teeth. It is kind of a funny-- because we had a very good radio at home. Telephones in the night could easily get the stations, whatever I wanted in Europe. But it just was not taken seriously. People did not want to talk-- first of all, it was very hard to, to, to emigrate. Most counties were closed. Israel was mandated. So you had to wait. Some people did get away to Israel. But you had to wait for the certificate for many, many years. And there were not, not many choices, except when the war started, Polish army left Poland for Romania. And some other people joined them, too. So that was one, one way to, to go. The Russian border was closed for past 20 years. There was no, no exchange whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear Hitler speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I, I heard him. What-- I don't remember was it-- I knew how he looked like. Yeah, some of his speeches were on, on, on newsreel. And, and then after-- they were other things which pointed to war, like Ribbentrop flew to-- was it Ribbentrop?-- to, to London. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we took it lightly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- do you remember what happened when you found out that the German army had invaded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we knew before because there was mobilization. And trains were full of, of young people, young men going to the front. But it was still not taken seriously

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day that you found out that Hitler had invaded? Do you remember your-- where you were, what you were doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I, I-- we were at this time in Rowne. And the cities were bombed. And we became [? swiped ?] with refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your city bombed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Rowne at this time was not bombed. Warsaw was bombed. And then, of course, on the radio, the war was announced.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the people around you react?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to dig shelters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was warm weather in September. And we had to-- there was constant [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] signals to, to, to, to duck in your room and to-- and to leave your building and go to shelters. But Rowne was not bombed at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a shelter, nevertheless?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to-- it usually was sometimes during the day time, so we did go. But people still did not take it seriously. Can I? Can I? I make mistake. It was not really Ribbentropp who went to London. It was Hess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rudolf Hess.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Rudolf Hess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who flew to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ribbentrop visited.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, he visited earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you-- can that be erased?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine. No problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, that's correct.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because it was Hess who went.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was Rudolf Hess who landed in England.

speaker: interviewee
text: In England. And he was trying make the peace, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear about that on the radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard it on the radio. It was in the papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was in the papers, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think that meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it, it was obvious that the war is coming. But still, people were-- at least people I knew-- were so used to peace, that-- though some already got mobilized. That they-- there was no particular panic, except from the cities like Warsaw, which was bombed and the refugees came east, mostly to Rowne. Rowne was known because of its prosperity. And also, it has Eastern fair, international fair. Like in fall, they had many-- there was a special exhibit. And many international people would come. So Rowne became known. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember what you were doing when you first saw the German army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, German army never eastern-- never entered the eastern Poland. Because according to Ribbentrop-Molotov Pact, they divided Poland. So after-- the war lasted only, I don't know, one week or 10 days. And then just obviously, the Germans were already-- in a few days, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was the Russians that you first saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All of a sudden, the border was closed for 20 years. And all of a sudden, they came with the tanks. And I saw. That was a total surprise.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did everyone react?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people felt that it's better than the Germans. And there was this joke that we were-- we expected a death sentence, but we ended with, with life imprisonment. So-- and of course, they started their-- the new policies right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, they-- they-- first the army moved, then the civilian administrators, teachers. They needed places for their family. You have to make space for them. And then, of course, KGB, which was called at this time and covered there-- came, that the [INAUDIBLE], that policy-- police. And they start examining everybody's past. And they-- and that took a couple of months, the most prominent people in politics. They were first taken in prison. And the others had to wait their turn. And then the families were sent to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they learn about the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they, they worked day and night in this building. And of course, they were probably examining income tax, which existed in Poland. And if you were a [INAUDIBLE]. Some people were known, mostly Jewish people, but also Polish people who were in like-- if they did not fled by then, they were also threatened and taken to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they interview you, the Russians, the Russian KGB?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. They did not actually. They had many informers watching for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you a number, or a card, or an identification?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we eventually got all the-- everybody in Russia has to have a passport. And we eventually got it. And there are many numbers in passport. We got number 11 written in our passport, which, at beginning, we didn't know what it means. But then, then they let us know that we have to leave Rowne because we are above, above the-- economical-- above the limit money-wise, that we are kind of a declared enemy of the people. And we have to go to a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Dubno. That was the very first year, where the Russians came in '39, of course, so when it was divided along the border. And already at the beginning of spring, in fall-- that was in the [? fall, ?] we had to leave. We could take only our-- I mean, everything, all the businesses which belonged to my father got nationalized. And-- but we could take our personal possession, also the money in bank and so forth, and whatever little furniture that they considered necessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you live in Dubno?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dubno, well, at the beginning, there were other people like us. There became an acute shortage of housing, because nothing was built but a lot of refugee, a lot of family of military came. And we had a hard time to find a dwelling, a place to live, even though we all live in one room. We all live in one room, except not a spacious house, not like in our spacious house in Dubno. Eventually, we got place, very small one. And it took a lot of effort. And I started Russian school. Everybody had to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the language of instructions overnight became Russian. And they had their own idea about teaching. There was very little foreign language but a lot of algebra. And all the teachers were Russian-- most of them, at least, were Russians. So I had to start school in a-- in a new surrounding in Dubno. But it was not that demanding, because I went before to a good school, so I had good background.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find that there was any different or special kind of instruction in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everything was, of course, against, against bourgeois, which we were. But I did not feel that personally. But there was a lot about Marx and Lenin, political instructions which you had to take if you want it or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel there was antisemitism involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Antisemitism at this time was not involved, because the schools were now open to everybody. So actually, the kids got along fine. But we are very battered and bored with this political indoctrination.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did get a political indoctrination?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Even history was interpreted in different sense than we had before, to-- than, than we were used to. I remember we had a history teacher. And his subject was French Revolution. So it was a very different interpretation what I read before and after.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you had to-- you had to make the grade. I read so many books and seen so many movies about-- even the American movie, was old American movie with Norma Shearer and Tyrone Power. And even that was more close. And it was romanticized and not right actually historically. But I-- many American movies actually were coming to Dubno. So even that was closer to those, what we got in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was-- what-- what was the Jewish community in Dubno?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they, they, they, they were doing quite all right. There were a lot of [? hop ?] plantation, business people, and a lot of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] prosperous people. So also, by time we got to Dubno, the people who had most money were already taken away and sent, sent to prison. Family might have been sent to Siberia or any other remote place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where they were sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the time, people knew because they were sending-- they did not know about the men. But they knew about the families, because I think some of them were allowed to have packages sent because the food was so meager in, in those remote places. Although if we get farther south, like Tashkent, it was a little easier than father north. It depends, but the food was always in demand. The Russians were buying actually everything. They were the best consumers ever existed. Yeah, so mostly whatever they could get, because-- and we became frightened about all those [NON-ENGLISH] in Russia. But of course, the war was-- that was this funny war, was still going on. So nobody knew much.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did actually have food shortages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Dubno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dubno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it's a very agricultural part of Poland. And there was enough of dairy products and also vegetables and fruit. It's a wooded area. We had a lot of various berries that were collected. There was still an open market, so you could go and buy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember what you were thinking about the future at that time? Did you see the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in school, that-- they were the last years of school, so I was thinking more, you know, that after the school, most of the kids do go to university. So I had to work hard. My father was helping me with-- we were thinking-- I was thinking to apply for mathematics, something like that, which was kind a neutral. We became aware of how vulnerable people are in Russia in various positions, that they could be in a high position one day and then the next-- and then-- and then they disappeared. And nobody knows why. So mathematics seemed to be-- algebra, or maybe physics, or whatever, seemed to be a safe profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you finish high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I-- the war started-- I was just about to finish the high school when the war started. So I-- officially I didn't ever finish it. And that was when-- when this war between Poland-- between Russia and Germany started. I think it was called Barbarossa, Operation Barbarossa after this pirate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could not get any further education?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because things dramatically changed overnight. Dubno is located on the main road between Lwow and Kiev. This road was actually-- it was always a very bad road. But the Russians built it to a new road. And that was the road that German tanks used to go to Kiev, because it went Lwow, Dubno, Rowne, and then into the Kiev. Before, it was just, in spring or in fall with rain, the road was very hard to get through. But now they put-- it was only two lane. Nevertheless, it was a big luxury for the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you had been invaded by the Russians and now by the Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you react to these re-invasions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were actually-- it was actually frightful, because they start taking people, Jewish people. Ukrainian were quite glad that it happened. They greeted them even. But for Jewish people, it started right away. My father was killed in Dubno one month after, after the German came. They, they did not-- they just collected at random hundred people, men. And they shot them. So it became frightful. And then from then on, these things were happening all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the, the-- what happened that day when your father was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were even not home. I got a short story here. I-- actually, we-- he-- we should have been known, because Gestapo came to town, several cars of it. But I went swimming with my friend. Actually, we had to right away those ID on our-- those yellow patches. But we decided to go swimming anyway. And when I was on this beach, we saw some Gestapo men, but we were so ignorant that we did not know who they are, although I read it in books. I read books by German writers before the war, and-- like, like Anna Seghers. It's a very famous German writer, who is now more popular than she was before. But we took it lightly. And when they came back, my brother told me, he was running already to a beach, that 100 men-- that one man escape. About 100 men were taken. One man escaped. They were said they going to do some work, but that was toward the evening. And he said that there was no work, just they were taken outside of town. And a big pit was dug out. And everybody was shot with, with a few exceptions. This man was photographer. And he said-- well, actually he spoke that he was photographer. And they let him go. And then the next-- we were not allowed to leave city after 7 o'clock. Nevertheless, in the next few days, peasant woman came with pieces of clothing that leaves no doubt that they were all shot. And there was, of course, you could see that the pit was filled, fresh earth. But still, people had delusions that maybe some of them were taken somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you first-- you first heard that your father was taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes

speaker: interviewer
text: --you didn't know quite what had happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or you had heard the rumors about the people being shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, he was one of them, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't know that till?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the next few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next few days.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to get some water. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family react to this horrible news?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the news repeated itself. Later, I think one week later, there came-- there came another transport of Gestapo And they took-- this time they took more people, I think 200 people. But they just-- they took them just at random, from house to house, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that work, they took a person or everybody in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took all, all men at this time, and they-- actually very simple. They-- my grandfather was taken. And they just repeated the whole thing. And people still believed that, that this-- [INAUDIBLE], was still naive about the whole thing. So very soon, many men from Dubno were gone, were shot, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: This must have been terrifying.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And then-- and then we decided to move to Lwow. Because I don't know why, it always was thought that that would be a safer place than staying in Dubno, in Dubno.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did have freedom of movement then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we did not. But somebody help us. You could not leave ghetto. You could not leave. You had to stay where you are usually. And there was a curfew at 7 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what you're saying is in Dubno, it became a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto became later. They let actually people still live in their places through the winter. And ghetto became in spring.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had gone on to Lwow after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Lwow, which that was illusion. We thought it would be more safe. We had some friends. But it really was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: The experience you had in Lwow, can you tell us how your family, the-- those that were left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the things more or-- more or less repeated itself. We knew in Lwow there was police-- police was every-- Jewish police in ghetto was a very-- very powerful force. Like in large ghetto, there was this famous man, Rumkowski. In Lwow, there was a man by name Boris Rojzen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boris Rojze, R-O-- R-O-J-Z-E-N. And he was actually from Rowne. And the Russians were after him. But he fled. He was hiding in our house. But then he fled. And they never found him. And then, again, he came to Lwow. He must have been close to a German side and then had some good connections. And then we found he-- that he's a police chief in Lwow. And he, he and his wife and, and their child, we knew them from Rowne. So we saw that there were other people from Rowne living in Lwow. So we thought that might be safer. But--
